questions.stem: A registered nurse is delegating morning care tasks to a nursing assistant (CNA). Which task is MOST appropriate to delegate?

options.text: Assess a new patient's skin for pressure injuries during the bed bath
Provide oral care to an unconscious patient on a ventilator
Instruct a patient with newly diagnosed diabetes about foot care
Measure and record vital signs for a stable post-operative patient on day 3

questions.rationale: Measuring and recording vital signs for a stable patient is a routine task within the CNA's scope of practice and does not require nursing judgment for data collection (though the RN interprets the results). Skin assessment requires RN-level assessment skills. Oral care for an unconscious, ventilated patient involves airway risk management and should be performed by licensed nursing staff. Patient education—such as foot care for a diabetic—is an RN responsibility that cannot be delegated.

distractorRationales.a: Assess a new patient's skin for pressure injuries during the bed bath misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Delegation airway points to Measure and record vital signs for a stable post-operative patient on day 3, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.b: Provide oral care to an unconscious patient on a ventilator misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Delegation airway points to Measure and record vital signs for a stable post-operative patient on day 3, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Instruct a patient with newly diagnosed diabetes about foot care misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Delegation airway points to Measure and record vital signs for a stable post-operative patient on day 3, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Delegate tasks that are routine, do not require nursing judgment, and involve stable patients. Assessment, education, and care requiring clinical judgment remain with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient's pulmonary artery catheter readings show: PA pressure 42/22 mmHg, PCWP 24 mmHg, CVP 14 mmHg, and cardiac output 3.2 L/min. The nurse correctly interprets these findings as indicative of:

options.text: Right ventricular failure with fluid overload
Left ventricular failure with elevated filling pressures
Hypovolemia with compensatory tachycardia
Normal hemodynamic values within reference ranges

questions.rationale: Normal PCWP is 6–12 mmHg. A wedge pressure of 24 mmHg (>18 mmHg) indicates elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, consistent with left ventricular failure or cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The elevated PA pressures reflect backward transmission of elevated LV pressures. Low cardiac output (normal 4–8 L/min) further confirms reduced LV performance. CVP of 14 mmHg (normal 2–8 mmHg) reflects right heart congestion secondary to LV failure.

distractorRationales.a: Right ventricular failure with fluid overload misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals pulmonary points to Left ventricular failure with elevated filling pressures, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Hypovolemia with compensatory tachycardia misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals pulmonary points to Left ventricular failure with elevated filling pressures, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Normal hemodynamic values within reference ranges misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals pulmonary points to Left ventricular failure with elevated filling pressures, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: PCWP >18 mmHg = LV failure / cardiogenic pulmonary edema. PCWP <6 mmHg = hypovolemia.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: Patient A (83 yo) has sudden-onset inattention, waxing-and-waning confusion, and visual hallucinations appearing 2 days after starting opioids post-hip surgery. Patient B (79 yo) has 4 years of progressive memory loss, gets lost in familiar environments, and repeatedly asks the same questions. Which statement correctly classifies these presentations?

options.text: Both have dementia; Patient A has early-onset and Patient B has late-stage Alzheimer's disease
Patient A has delirium (acute, fluctuating, reversible, identifiable cause); Patient B has dementia (insidious, progressive, irreversible)
Patient A has depression (pseudodementia) from opioid use; Patient B has Lewy body dementia
Both require CT head imaging to differentiate — clinical assessment alone is insufficient

questions.rationale: Delirium vs Dementia: Delirium — ACUTE onset (hours to days), FLUCTUATING course, typically reversible with treatment of precipitant (opioids, infection, metabolic derangement, post-op state), hallucinations (especially visual) common, INATTENTION is the cardinal distinguishing feature. Dementia — INSIDIOUS onset (months to years), PROGRESSIVE course, irreversible underlying pathology, recent memory loss predominates, attention relatively preserved until late stages. Patient A has delirium (acute, precipitant-identified — opioids) and Patient B has dementia (gradual progression). Treatment of delirium: remove or reduce offending drug, provide reorientation, preserve sleep-wake cycle, family presence, early mobility; low-dose haloperidol for severe agitation. Delirium in hospitalized elderly is associated with significantly increased mortality and prolonged hospitalization. Use CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) to screen.

distractorRationales.a: Both have dementia; Patient A has early-onset and Patient B has late-stage Alzheimer's disease misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology infection points to Patient A has delirium (acute, fluctuating, reversible, identifiable cause); Patient B has dementia (insidious, progressive, irreversible), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Patient A has depression (pseudodementia) from opioid use; Patient B has Lewy body dementia misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology infection points to Patient A has delirium (acute, fluctuating, reversible, identifiable cause); Patient B has dementia (insidious, progressive, irreversible), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Both require CT head imaging to differentiate — clinical assessment alone is insufficient misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology infection points to Patient A has delirium (acute, fluctuating, reversible, identifiable cause); Patient B has dementia (insidious, progressive, irreversible), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Delirium: acute onset, fluctuating, reversible, identifiable cause, inattention is cardinal. Dementia: insidious, progressive, irreversible. Delirium and dementia can coexist — dementia is the #1 risk factor for delirium.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with acute decompensated heart failure who has a new order for furosemide 40 mg IV push. Which assessment finding is MOST important to report to the provider before administering this medication?

options.text: The patient's weight is 4 lbs above their dry weight
The patient's serum potassium is 3.0 mEq/L
The patient has bilateral 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities
The patient reports urinary urgency

questions.rationale: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that causes potassium wasting. A pre-dose potassium of 3.0 mEq/L (hypokalemia) is a critical safety concern — administering furosemide will further lower potassium, increasing the risk of life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias (torsades de pointes, ventricular fibrillation), especially in heart failure patients who may be on digoxin. Potassium replacement should be confirmed or ordered before or concurrent with diuresis. Weight gain above dry weight and bilateral edema are the indications FOR furosemide, not contraindications. Urinary urgency is an expected diuretic side effect.

distractorRationales.a: The patient's weight is 4 lbs above their dry weight misses the clinical pattern: Cardiovascular / Heart Failure edema points to The patient's serum potassium is 3.0 mEq/L, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: The patient has bilateral 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities misses the clinical pattern: Cardiovascular / Heart Failure edema points to The patient's serum potassium is 3.0 mEq/L, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: The patient reports urinary urgency misses the clinical pattern: Cardiovascular / Heart Failure edema points to The patient's serum potassium is 3.0 mEq/L, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Before furosemide: check K+. If < 3.5 mEq/L, notify provider and hold or replace K+ first. Diuretics + hypokalemia = arrhythmia risk. This is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.

visualRationale.title: Cardiovascular / Heart Failure
visualRationale.caption: Before furosemide: check K+. If < 3.5 mEq/L, notify provider and hold or replace K+ first. Diuretics + hypokalemia = arrhythmia risk. This is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.
visualRationale.conclusion: Before furosemide: check K+. If < 3.5 mEq/L, notify provider and hold or replace K+ first. Diuretics + hypokalemia = arrhythmia risk. This is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is admitted with RSV bronchiolitis. SpO2 is 91% on room air, RR 58, mild intercostal retractions, poor feeding. The physician orders albuterol. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options.text: Administer albuterol as ordered; it is the standard of care for RSV bronchiolitis
Question the order; bronchodilators are not recommended as routine treatment for bronchiolitis — prioritize nasal suctioning and supportive care
Hold the albuterol and administer corticosteroids instead
Administer albuterol only if SpO2 drops below 88%

questions.rationale: RSV bronchiolitis is managed with supportive care only per AAP guidelines: nasal suctioning (most effective intervention for symptom relief), adequate hydration (NG feeds if needed), and supplemental O2 for SpO2 <90–92%. Bronchodilators (albuterol) and corticosteroids are NOT recommended because bronchiolitis involves viral-induced inflammation and mucus plugging — not bronchospasm — making bronchodilators ineffective. Multiple RCTs have shown no benefit. The nurse should question the order and clarify before administration. Contact precautions (RSV is spread via droplet/contact) are required.

distractorRationales.a: Administer albuterol as ordered; it is the standard of care for RSV bronchiolitis misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing spo2 points to Question the order; bronchodilators are not recommended as routine treatment for bronchiolitis — prioritize nasal suctioning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Hold the albuterol and administer corticosteroids instead misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing spo2 points to Question the order; bronchodilators are not recommended as routine treatment for bronchiolitis — prioritize nasal suctioning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Administer albuterol only if SpO2 drops below 88% misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing spo2 points to Question the order; bronchodilators are not recommended as routine treatment for bronchiolitis — prioritize nasal suctioning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.
visualRationale.conclusion: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.

questions.stem: A patient arrives in the PACU after 2.5-hour general anesthesia for abdominal surgery. SpO2 is 88%, respiratory rate is 5 breaths/min and shallow, the patient is unresponsive to voice, and ETCO2 is 72 mmHg. What is the priority nursing action?

options.text: Verbally and physically stimulate the patient repeatedly and wait 5 minutes for residual anesthesia to clear
Elevate HOB to 30° and apply supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula at 4 L/min
Open the airway with a jaw thrust, assist ventilation with 100% O2 via bag-valve-mask, prepare reversal agents (naloxone or sugammadex as appropriate), and prepare for reintubation if no improvement
Notify the surgeon and anesthesiologist and await orders before intervening

questions.rationale: Post-anesthesia respiratory depression is a PACU emergency. SpO2 88% + RR 5 + unresponsive + ETCO2 72 mmHg = severe hypoventilation with CO2 retention — caused by residual opioids, residual neuromuscular blockade, or residual volatile agents. This requires IMMEDIATE active intervention: (1) Airway — jaw thrust to relieve obstruction in the obtunded patient; (2) Breathing — 100% O2 via bag-valve-mask with assisted ventilation (nasal cannula at 4 L/min provides approximately 36% FiO2 and no ventilatory support — completely inadequate); (3) Reversal agents — naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV for opioid-induced respiratory depression (titrate slowly to avoid acute agitation and pain); sugammadex 200 mg (or 16 mg/kg) for rocuronium/vecuronium reversal; flumazenil for benzodiazepines; (4) Prepare for reintubation if no response. The PACU nurse must act — immediate physician notification is also required, but waiting for orders before airway management is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: Verbally and physically stimulate the patient repeatedly and wait 5 minutes for residual anesthesia to clear misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care airway points to Open the airway with a jaw thrust, assist ventilation with 100% O2 via bag-valve-mask, prepare reversal, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.b: Elevate HOB to 30° and apply supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula at 4 L/min misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care airway points to Open the airway with a jaw thrust, assist ventilation with 100% O2 via bag-valve-mask, prepare reversal, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Notify the surgeon and anesthesiologist and await orders before intervening misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care airway points to Open the airway with a jaw thrust, assist ventilation with 100% O2 via bag-valve-mask, prepare reversal, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: PACU respiratory emergency: SpO2 low + RR <8 + unresponsive + high ETCO2 = hypoventilation. Jaw thrust + BVM + 100% O2 immediately. Consider reversal agents (naloxone, sugammadex). Prepare to reintubate.

questions.stem: A nurse is providing discharge teaching to a patient newly prescribed levothyroxine (Synthroid) for hypothyroidism. Which instruction is MOST important to include?

options.text: Take the medication with a full meal to improve absorption
Take the medication on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast with water, and avoid taking it with calcium or iron supplements
Double the dose if a dose is missed to maintain thyroid hormone levels
Expect to see symptom improvement within 24-48 hours of starting therapy

questions.rationale: Levothyroxine absorption is significantly reduced by food, calcium, iron supplements, antacids containing calcium/magnesium, and several other medications. The medication should be taken on an empty stomach (30-60 minutes before breakfast) with a full glass of water. Calcium and iron supplements should be taken at least 4 hours apart from levothyroxine. Doubling a missed dose is incorrect and dangerous — skip the missed dose and continue at the regular schedule. Symptom improvement takes weeks to months (TSH normalization is checked at 4-6 weeks after dose adjustment), not 24-48 hours.

distractorRationales.a: Take the medication with a full meal to improve absorption misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Endocrine / Levothyroxine Teaching calcium points to Take the medication on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast with water, and avoid taking, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.c: Double the dose if a dose is missed to maintain thyroid hormone levels misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Endocrine / Levothyroxine Teaching calcium points to Take the medication on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast with water, and avoid taking, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Expect to see symptom improvement within 24-48 hours of starting therapy misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Endocrine / Levothyroxine Teaching calcium points to Take the medication on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast with water, and avoid taking, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Levothyroxine: empty stomach + water + 30-60 min before breakfast. Separate from calcium, iron, antacids by 4h. Never double dose. Onset of therapeutic effect: 4-6 weeks for TSH normalization. Compliance is key — once-daily dosing forever.

questions.subcategory: Safety and Infection Control / Stable vs. Unstable
questions.stem: A nurse is triaging patients calling the unit. Which patient should the nurse advise to come in for evaluation IMMEDIATELY versus scheduling a same-day appointment?

options.text: A 68-year-old with known hypertension reporting a new headache, sudden in onset, 10/10 severity, that 'feels different from any headache I've ever had'
A 45-year-old with migraines reporting a typical headache with associated nausea, onset gradual, 7/10
A 52-year-old requesting a refill of their blood pressure medication
A 77-year-old with osteoarthritis reporting increased knee pain after gardening

questions.rationale: A sudden-onset, maximal-intensity headache described as 'the worst of my life' or 'different from any previous headache' is a classic presentation of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) until proven otherwise. This is a neurological emergency with high mortality if not diagnosed and treated rapidly. The hallmark is the 'thunderclap' sudden onset reaching maximum intensity within seconds to minutes. The migraine patient has a familiar pattern, the BP refill is routine, and the arthritis flare is non-urgent. SAH screening requires emergent imaging.

distractorRationales.b: A 45-year-old with migraines reporting a typical headache with associated nausea, onset gradual, 7/10 misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Stable Vs. Unstable hemorrhage points to A 68-year-old with known hypertension reporting a new headache, sudden in onset, 10/10 severity, that 'feels, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: A 52-year-old requesting a refill of their blood pressure medication misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Stable Vs. Unstable hemorrhage points to A 68-year-old with known hypertension reporting a new headache, sudden in onset, 10/10 severity, that 'feels, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: A 77-year-old with osteoarthritis reporting increased knee pain after gardening misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Stable Vs. Unstable hemorrhage points to A 68-year-old with known hypertension reporting a new headache, sudden in onset, 10/10 severity, that 'feels, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

tags: Safety and Infection Control / Stable vs. Unstable
questions.takeaway: The 'thunderclap headache'—sudden onset, maximal severity, described as the worst ever—is SAH until proven otherwise. Always direct these patients to emergency evaluation immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with type 2 diabetes takes empagliflozin (Jardiance) and is scheduled for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy tomorrow. The anesthesiologist orders 'hold all diabetes medications the morning of surgery.' Which additional concern should the nurse identify and report?

options.text: SGLT2 inhibitors should be held 3-4 days before surgery, not just the morning of, due to risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis
Empagliflozin has no pre-surgical risks and can be given the morning of surgery safely
The patient should receive double the dose of empagliflozin pre-operatively to prevent hyperglycemia
SGLT2 inhibitors should be switched to metformin 24 hours before surgery

questions.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin) carry a risk of euglycemic DKA — a form of DKA where blood glucose can be near-normal or only mildly elevated, making it easy to miss. This risk is significantly increased perioperatively due to fasting, surgical stress, and altered carbohydrate metabolism. Current guidelines (ADA, FDA, ERAS protocols) recommend holding SGLT2 inhibitors 3-4 days before elective surgery. Holding only the morning of surgery is insufficient. The nurse should escalate this finding to the anesthesiologist and surgeon for clarification and an updated order to hold the medication earlier. This is an emerging but increasingly important perioperative safety issue.

distractorRationales.b: Empagliflozin has no pre-surgical risks and can be given the morning of surgery safely misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Endocrine / SGLT2 Inhibitors glucose points to SGLT2 inhibitors should be held 3-4 days before surgery, not just the morning of, due to, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: The patient should receive double the dose of empagliflozin pre-operatively to prevent hyperglycemia misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Endocrine / SGLT2 Inhibitors glucose points to SGLT2 inhibitors should be held 3-4 days before surgery, not just the morning of, due to, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: SGLT2 inhibitors should be switched to metformin 24 hours before surgery misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Endocrine / SGLT2 Inhibitors glucose points to SGLT2 inhibitors should be held 3-4 days before surgery, not just the morning of, due to, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins) + surgery = euglycemic DKA risk. Hold 3-4 days before elective procedures. Euglycemic DKA is dangerous because normal blood glucose masks the diagnosis. Key teaching: fasting + SGLT2 inhibitor = starvation ketosis + insulin deficiency = DKA without classic hyperglycemia.
